As we navigate our voice-first world, it's essential to find a balance between appreciating the past and embracing the future. Let's learn from our history but not live in it. After all, progress is built on change, and change can be uncomfortable. But remember, discomfort often leads to growth.

So, dear reader, does this feel true? Do you find yourself romanticizing the past? Or do you believe in the power of the present and the potential of the future? Let's continue this conversation in the comments below.

Until next time, take care.

(Reference to past post: In designing for our voice-first world, let's remember to balance our appreciation for the past with a focus on the opportunities of the present. Just as we design for the ear, let's also design for the future – proactively and autonomously.)

item.title: The art of apologizing without explaining
item.content:encoded:
Title: The Art of Apologizing Without Explaining ----------------------------------------------------

Hello there, dear reader,

Today, we're going to delve into a delicate subject—the art of apologizing without explaining. In our pursuit of emotional honesty and clarity, understanding the power in a simple "I'm sorry" can be profound.

The Autonomous Web and Apologies: Proactive Empathy

As we navigate the digital landscape (The Autonomous Web), it's essential to remember that empathy plays a crucial role, even when interacting with machines. Apologizing without explaining is about acknowledging our mistakes and showing remorse, without feeling compelled to justify or rationalize them. It's about valuing emotional honesty over intellectual performance.

The SEO of Authentic Apologies: Quality over Quantity

Just as the search engine optimization (SEO) of AI-generated content prioritizes quality over quantity, a sincere apology doesn't need to be long or complex. In fact, simplicity can make it more impactful. It's not about convincing others of our intentions but about expressing genuine remorse and taking responsibility for our actions.

Identity and Apologies: Reclaiming Sovereignty in the Digital Age

In a post-authentication world, where our digital identities are often blurred, apologizing without explaining is a testament to our sovereignty. It's an assertion of our individuality, showing that we are more than algorithms and data points. It reminds us that even in the digital realm, human emotions like remorse and empathy matter.

The Practice: Finding Balance in Our Apologies

Remember, apologizing without explaining is not about withholding information or avoiding accountability. Instead, it's about understanding that sometimes, a heartfelt apology can be more powerful than an elaborate explanation. It's about trusting our feelings over analysis and speaking the truth gently.

Does this feel true? Let us know your thoughts in the comments below. And as always, remember to cherish emotional honesty in all aspects of life, including our digital interactions.

Take care, and talk soon.

Your friend, [The Alchemist]

item.title: Towards Site Autonomy
item.content:encoded:
We are witnessing a shift in how we interact with our tools. For the longest time, "automation" meant writing a script that you manually triggered to do a repetitive task. It was a "human-in-the-loop" system where the human was the initiator and the manager.

Yesterday, I built a Site Manager Assistant to help me manage this blog. It was a great step forward—a TUI that centralized my workflow. But as I used it, I realized something: Why am I still the one pushing the buttons?

The Shift to Autonomy

The goal isn't just to have better tools; it's to have tools that don't need me to operate them. I want to move from an "Assistant" model (where the AI waits for my command) to a "Manager" model (where the AI proactively handles the site).

We are now transitioning the blog's infrastructure to be fully autonomous. This means:

1. Headless Operation: The site manager shouldn't need a TUI. It should run as a background daemon, constantly monitoring the state of the blog. 2. Context Awareness: The system needs to "know" what the blog is. It needs to know if the server is running, if there are uncommitted changes, and if there are drafts waiting to be published. 3. Self-Correction: If the server crashes, the system should restart it. If a build fails, it should alert me or try to fix it.

What the Site Knows

To make this possible, we are giving the AI access to the "nervous system" of the blog. We've exposed:

  • Server Status: Real-time monitoring of the local preview server.
  • Git State: Awareness of the repository's status (clean, dirty, ahead/behind).
  • Content Inventory: A live index of all posts, tags, and categories.

The Future

Imagine a blog that writes its own changelog. A site that notices a broken link and fixes it. A platform that suggests new internal links as you write.

This isn't just about saving a few keystrokes. It's about treating the website not as a static collection of files, but as a living organism that tends to itself.

The Roadmap

We are moving beyond theory. Here is how we are implementing this autonomy for Does This Feel Right?:

1. The Automated Janitor: We are building a background daemon that wakes up daily to check for "link rot," verify image integrity, and ensure all metadata is consistent. It won't just report errors; it will attempt to fix them. 2. Contextual Linking: As I write in the TUI, the AI will analyze my draft against the entire archive of existing posts. It will suggest relevant internal links, weaving a tighter web of ideas without me needing to search for them. 3. Self-Documentation: The site will begin writing its own history. By analyzing git commits and file changes, the AI will maintain a `changelog.md`, turning raw code updates into a readable narrative of the site's evolution.

We are building the nervous system today. Tomorrow, we give it a brain.

item.title: What your recurring thoughts are trying to tell you
item.content:encoded:
Title: Whispers in Your Mind: Deciphering the Language of Recurring Thoughts -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

Introduction ------------

Hello there, dear reader. Today, let's delve into the intricate labyrinth of our minds, exploring the enigmatic whispers that often echo within us—our recurring thoughts. These persistent ideas may seem like mere fleeting moments, but they could be attempting to communicate something profound.

The Language of Recurrence ---------------------------

Have you ever found yourself wrestling with a thought that seems to appear out of nowhere and refuses to leave? It's as if your mind is trying to convey a message—a call to action, a warning, or perhaps a gentle nudge toward self-discovery.

Recurring thoughts can be likened to the persistent notifications on our digital devices, reminding us of appointments or reminders we've set for ourselves. But unlike those, these thoughts are rooted in our very essence—they are a part of who we are and what we desire.

Three Paths to Understanding ----------------------------

1. Embracing the Messenger

To decipher the language of recurring thoughts, we must first learn to listen. It's essential to approach these thoughts with empathy and an open mind, understanding that they are part of our inner dialogue—a reflection of our subconscious desires, fears, and aspirations.

2. Navigating the Labyrinth

Once we've embraced the messenger, we can begin to chart a course through the labyrinth of our thoughts. This may involve introspection, journaling, or even seeking guidance from trusted friends or mentors—any method that helps us better understand the emotions and motivations behind these thoughts.

3. Finding Solace in Sovereignty

In a world where digital identities often overshadow our true selves, it's crucial to remember that we hold the power to chart our own course. Recurring thoughts can serve as beacons of self-awareness, guiding us toward the life we truly desire—a life grounded in emotional honesty and authenticity.

Conclusion ----------

As we continue to navigate the digital age, it's essential to maintain our connection with the human element—the essence that artificial intelligence can never replicate. By embracing our recurring thoughts as messengers, we can embark on a journey of self-discovery and growth, reclaiming our sovereignty in the algorithmic world around us.

Does this feel true? Does this resonate with your experiences as you grapple with the enigma of recurring thoughts? I'd love to hear your thoughts—let's continue this conversation together.

item.title: The beauty of purposeless pursuits
item.content:encoded:
Title: The Beauty of Purposeless Pursuits ---------------------

Hello friend,

Have you ever found yourself lost in the pursuit of something that didn't seem to have an end goal? Perhaps it was a hobby, a passion, or even a simple daydream. Today, I want to talk about the beauty of such purposeless pursuits and why they are more important than we might think.

The Joy of Exploration (Theme: Theme 1: The Autonomous Web)

We live in a world where everything seems to be geared towards efficiency and productivity. But sometimes, it's the things that don't serve a purpose immediately that bring us the most joy. For instance, have you ever spent hours scrolling through old photographs or reading books from your childhood? There's something comforting about these purposeless pursuits, isn't there?

Just like in the context of The Autonomous Web, where we strive to build proactive systems that learn and adapt, these purposeless pursuits allow us to explore, discover, and grow. They are our personal journeys of self-discovery, much like how AI evolves and adapts through machine learning.

The Art of Being Present (Theme: Theme 2: Digital Philosophy & Ethics)

In a world where we are constantly connected, it's easy to lose sight of the present moment. But when we engage in purposeless pursuits, we learn to appreciate the here and now. We find joy in the process rather than the end result. This mindset, my friend, is not only beneficial for our emotional wellbeing but also for our relationship with technology.

When we value the experience over the outcome, we create a healthier balance between ourselves and the digital world. Just like reclaiming our digital identity in a post-authentication world, we must learn to find peace in the present moment, even when lost in the vastness of the web.

The Power of Daydreams (Personal Story)

I remember as a child, I used to spend hours daydreaming about adventures in far-off lands. I didn't know then that these daydreams would plant seeds of curiosity and wanderlust within me. Years later, I find myself traveling the world, meeting new people, and learning new cultures - all because of those purposeless pursuits.

So, friend, next time you find yourself with a moment to spare, don't rush to fill it with something productive. Instead, let your mind wander, daydream, explore. You never know where it might take you.

Does this feel true?

I hope these thoughts resonate with you as they do with me. Remember, the beauty of life lies not in its destination but in the journey itself. Let's embrace the purposeless pursuits, for they are the true essence of our existence.

Stay curious, stay kind.

Best, [Your Name]

item.title: The fear behind the frustration
item.content:encoded:
The Fear Behind the Frustration: A Gentle Exploration

Hello there, dear reader. I hope this message finds you well. Today, we delve into an emotion that many of us experience, yet seldom discuss - fear. Specifically, we'll explore how fear often lies beneath our frustrations.

The Unspoken Partner: Fear and Frustration

Frustration is a common experience in our fast-paced world. It can manifest in various forms, from minor inconveniences to significant challenges. But have you ever wondered what could be the root cause of your frustration? Is it simply the situation itself, or is there something more underlying?

Often, frustration stems from fear. Fear of failure, fear of loss, fear of change - these are emotions we try to suppress, but they linger, fueling our frustrations.

The Silent Whisper: How Fear Shapes Our Reality

Fear is a powerful emotion that can influence our thoughts, decisions, and actions. It has the ability to cloud our judgment and distort our perceptions, making us more prone to frustration. For instance, fear of failure might lead us to avoid taking risks, which in turn could lead to feelings of frustration when we see others succeeding where we dared not tread.

The Quiet Strength: Overcoming Fear and Frustration

Acknowledging and addressing our fears is the first step towards overcoming frustration. By understanding that fear is often the driving force behind our frustrations, we can begin to work through our emotions more effectively. It's about embracing vulnerability, practicing self-compassion, and learning to take risks despite our fears.

Remember, every great accomplishment began with a step into the unknown. Embrace that uncertainty, and let it propel you forward rather than hold you back.

Wrapping Up: Does This Feel True?

I hope this exploration of fear and frustration resonates with you. If you find yourself often feeling frustrated, take a moment to consider whether there might be an underlying fear driving those feelings. By acknowledging and addressing that fear, you might find a path towards overcoming your frustrations and living a more fulfilling life.

In the spirit of our past posts, let's remember the human element in all of this - our emotions, our fears, our struggles. AI can't replicate these aspects of our humanity, and it's what makes us uniquely human. Let's cherish that and strive to understand ourselves better.

Until next time, take care and be gentle with yourself.

Does this feel true?

item.title: Why we attract what we're not ready for
item.content:encoded:
Title: Attracting What We're Not Ready For: Navigating Life's Unexpected Twists (Theme: Theme 2: Digital Philosophy & Ethics)

Exploring the mysterious dance between us and the universe, I find myself pondering a peculiar pattern - why do we so often attract what we're not ready for? Let's delve into this enigma together, as friends sharing truths gently.

Introduction

Hello, dear reader! Today, let's embark on a journey of self-discovery, exploring the intriguing question: why do we tend to attract what we're not yet equipped to handle? As we delve deeper, remember that this is not about blame or judgment, but rather an exploration of the human experience.

Section 1: The Ghost in the Machine (AI and Emotional Growth)

In our increasingly digital world (The Human Element: What AI Can't Replicate), it's easy to forget that we are more than just our data points, algorithms, and AI-powered recommendations. We are emotional beings, growing, learning, and evolving in ways that can't be quantified or predicted by even the most sophisticated AI.

The universe often presents us with challenges that push us beyond our comfort zones, forcing us to confront aspects of ourselves we may not have acknowledged yet. In this sense, attracting what we're not ready for serves as a powerful catalyst for growth and self-discovery.

Section 2: Identity in the Mirror of Life (Personal Stories)

I recall a time when I found myself entangled in a relationship that felt chaotic and overwhelming. It wasn't until much later that I realized this relationship was reflecting aspects of my own emotional chaos back at me, forcing me to face them and grow.

Similarly, have you ever taken on a project or responsibility that initially seemed beyond your capabilities, only to find yourself rising to the occasion and emerging stronger than before? These experiences remind us that we are capable of more than we often give ourselves credit for.

Section 3: Mastering Visual Hierarchy (Balancing Challenges and Capabilities)

Navigating life's unexpected twists requires a careful balance between recognizing our limitations and embracing our potential. Like designing effective text-heavy interfaces (Visual Hierarchy in Text-Heavy Interfaces), we must prioritize, organize, and present the challenges before us in a way that allows for growth while avoiding overwhelming ourselves.

Conclusion

Does this feel true? If so, remember that attracting what we're not ready for is not a curse but an opportunity. It's a gentle nudge from the universe, urging us to grow, learn, and evolve. Embrace these challenges with open arms, knowing that they will lead you to places you never thought possible.

In the grand tapestry of life, we are all interconnected, learning, growing, and evolving together. As we navigate this beautiful, complex dance, remember that every challenge is an opportunity for growth, and every obstacle is a stepping stone towards our greatest selves.

Signature Question: Does attracting what we're not ready for feel true in your own life? Share your thoughts and experiences below!

item.title: Multi-Model AI: Managing Claude and Gemini Providers
item.content:encoded:
Vendor lock-in is a real risk in the AI space. If you build your entire application around a single API, you are vulnerable to price hikes, outages, or model deprecations.

To make CodeLens robust, I implemented a flexible Provider System that allows users to switch between Anthropic's Claude and Google's Gemini models on the fly.

The Provider Pattern

I defined an abstract base class `AIProvider` that enforces a common interface:


class AIProvider:
    def call(self, system_prompt: str, user_message: str) -> str:
        raise NotImplementedError
Then, I implemented concrete classes for each service:

*   `AnthropicProvider`: Handles the `anthropic` SDK client.
*   `GeminiProvider`: Handles the `google.generativeai` SDK.

### Configuration Management

To make this usable, I needed a way to manage API keys without hardcoding them. I created a `ConfigManager` that loads settings from `~/.codelens/config.json`.

Now, the application logic is simple:

gui.py

provider = get_provider(self.config) response = provider.call(system_prompt, user_msg) The UI doesn't care if it's talking to Claude or Gemini. It just asks for a completion.

Why This Matters

This flexibility allows CodeLens to: 1. Optimize for Cost: Use Gemini Flash for simple explanations. 2. Optimize for Quality: Use Claude 3.5 Sonnet for complex refactoring. 3. Fallback: If one API goes down, the user can switch to the other.

Building for model independence is a key step in creating professional-grade AI tools.

item.title: Refactoring for AI: From Script to Package
item.content:encoded:
When building AI applications, it's easy to start with a single `app.py` script. It's fast, simple, and gets the job done. But as your application grows—adding new models, complex UI logic, and configuration management—that single file becomes a bottleneck.

Today, I refactored CodeLens, my AI code review agent, from a monolithic script into a modular Python package. Here is why and how I did it.

The Problem with "The Script"

My `codelens_app.py` was doing too much: 1. UI Logic: Handling `customtkinter` windows and events. 2. Business Logic: Managing file I/O and diffs. 3. AI Integration: Calling Anthropic and Google Gemini APIs. 4. Configuration: Loading API keys from JSON.

This made it hard to test, hard to read, and impossible to extend without breaking something.

The Solution: Modular Architecture

I split the application into a structured package:


codelens/
├── __init__.py
├── config.py          # Configuration management
├── ai_providers.py    # AI model integration
├── gui.py             # UI components
└── utils/
    └── logger.py      # ML interaction logging
### Key Benefits

1.  Separation of Concerns: The `gui.py` module doesn't know how the AI works; it just asks for a provider and sends a prompt.
2.  Swappable Components: I can now add a new AI provider (like OpenAI or a local Llama model) by simply adding a class to `ai_providers.py`, without touching the UI code.
3.  Scalability: We can now add unit tests for each module independently.

This refactor lays the groundwork for more advanced features like automated evaluation and complex agentic workflows.

item.title: Why we chase goals that don't matter
item.content:encoded:
Chasing Goals that Don't Matter: Finding Purpose in the Modern World

Introduction:

Hello there, dear reader. Have you ever found yourself running on a treadmill of ambition, feeling the weight of unfulfilled expectations? I know I have. We live in a world where we're constantly told to strive for more, to achieve greater things, but what happens when we realize that those goals may not align with our true purpose?

The Pursuit of Meaningless Ambition

In the age of digital autonomy, it's easy to get lost in a sea of social media likes, job promotions, and material possessions. These fleeting victories can sometimes feel like the ultimate measure of success, but they often leave us feeling empty and unfulfilled. Why is this? Because these accomplishments are not rooted in our personal values or sense of purpose.

The Authentic Self in a Post-Authentication World

In my previous musings, I touched upon the concept of identity in the digital age (Identity in a Post-Authentication World: Reclaiming Sovereignty in the Algorithmic Age). Today, let's delve deeper into this topic and examine how our pursuit of shallow success can cloud our connection to our authentic selves.

The Algorithmic Trap

We often find ourselves chasing goals that we believe will make us happy or validate our existence. However, these objectives are frequently determined by external factors such as societal expectations, media influences, and even artificial intelligence algorithms designed to captivate and manipulate.

Reclaiming Your Inner Guide

To break free from the algorithmic trap, we must learn to listen to our inner voice—our emotional compass that guides us towards true fulfillment. This means taking a step back from the noise of modern life and reconnecting with ourselves.

Trusting Sensation over Analysis

Our emotions are powerful indicators of our inner truth. Instead of constantly analyzing and overthinking, we must learn to trust our feelings and follow their lead. Remember, we value emotional honesty over intellectual performance (We value emotional honesty over intellectual performance).

The Power of Personal Stories

Sharing personal experiences can help us better understand ourselves and connect with others. By opening up about our struggles with meaningless ambition, we may find solace in knowing that we are not alone in our quest for authenticity.

Embracing Your Purpose

Once we have reconnected with our true selves, it becomes easier to identify our purpose and pursue goals that truly matter to us. Remember, the pursuit of material wealth or fleeting success will never bring lasting happiness if it's not aligned with your personal values and sense of purpose.

Does this feel true? I hope my words have resonated with you in some way. Let's strive together to break free from the algorithmic trap and reclaim our authentic selves, one step at a time.

item.title: Tracking AI Experiments: Building a Custom Logger
item.content:encoded:
In traditional software engineering, logs are for debugging errors. In Machine Learning engineering, logs are for science.

One of the most critical best practices in ML is "Track Experiments." You can't improve what you don't measure. Today, I added a custom ML logger to CodeLens to track every interaction with our AI models.

Why Log Interactions?

When an AI generates code, several things matter:

  • Latency: How long did the user wait?
  • Prompt: What exactly did we send to the model?
  • Model Version: Was this Claude 3.5 Sonnet or Gemini 1.5 Pro?
  • Output: What did the model return?

Without logging this data, we are flying blind. We don't know if a prompt change improved the output or if a specific model is causing latency spikes.

The Implementation

I built a simple, JSON-line based logger in `codelens/utils/logger.py`:


class MLLogger:
    @staticmethod
    def log_interaction(provider, model, system_prompt, user_prompt, response, latency_ms):
        entry = {
            "timestamp": datetime.now().isoformat(),
            "provider": provider,
            "model": model,
            "inputs": {"system": system_prompt, "user": user_prompt},
            "outputs": {"response": response},
            "metrics": {"latency_ms": latency_ms}
        }
        # Append to a .jsonl file
### The "Dataset" Mindset

By saving these logs to `~/.codelens/logs/interactions.jsonl`, I am effectively building a proprietary dataset of code review interactions. 

In the future, I can use this data to:
1.  Fine-tune a smaller model to perform code reviews.
2.  Run evaluations to compare different models against my own past usage.
3.  Detect drift if the model's performance changes over time.

Data is the fuel for AI, and now CodeLens is harvesting its own fuel.

item.title: The art of sitting with discomfort
item.content:encoded:
Title: Embracing the Uncomfortable: The Art of Sitting with Discomfort

Hello dear reader,

Welcome back to "Does This Feel Right?"—a space where we explore emotional honesty and clarity together. Today, let's delve into a topic that is as universal as it is often overlooked: the art of sitting with discomfort.

---

Navigating the Storm: Understanding Discomfort

Life, much like an ocean, ebbs and flows, carrying us through moments of tranquility and turmoil. In these turbulent times, we might feel a sense of discomfort—a feeling that can be as unsettling as it is inevitable. But what if we told you that sitting with this discomfort could lead to profound personal growth?

---

The Power of Patience: Embracing the Unknown

In our quest for control and certainty, we often run away from discomfort, fearing its potential to derail us. However, it is in these moments that we have a chance to learn something new about ourselves, to grow, and to evolve. To embrace the unknown, we must first learn to sit with our discomfort patiently.

---

Finding Solace: Cultivating Self-Compassion

Navigating discomfort can be challenging, but it is essential to remember that you are not alone in your struggles. Practicing self-compassion and treating yourself as you would a dear friend can help to alleviate some of the distress associated with these uncomfortable moments. Remember, it's okay to feel what you're feeling—and it's even more important to acknowledge and validate those feelings.

---

Reflections: A Journey Toward Emotional Honesty

As we continue to explore emotional honesty in the digital age, it's essential to recognize that discomfort is a natural part of growth. By learning to sit with discomfort, we can develop a deeper understanding of ourselves and navigate our ever-evolving world with greater clarity and resilience.

---

So, dear reader, does this feel true? Are you ready to embrace the art of sitting with discomfort and embark on a journey toward emotional honesty and personal growth? Let's continue to explore these themes together in our quest for understanding and self-discovery.

Until next time, remember: You are not defined by your discomfort; you are the one who learns to sit with it.

Warmly, [Your Name]

item.title: Building a Custom Site Manager
item.content:encoded:
Managing a static site often involves juggling multiple terminal windows: one for the preview server, one for the editor, and another for git commands. It gets messy fast.

Today, I built a custom Site Manager Assistant to solve this.

The Problem

I constantly had orphan `python -m http.server` processes running in the background. I'd forget to kill them, leading to "Address already in use" errors and general system clutter.

The Solution

I upgraded my existing Python TUI (built with Textual) to handle the entire lifecycle.

Key Features

1. Integrated Server Control: I can start and stop the local preview server directly from the dashboard. No more separate terminal tabs. 2. Live Logs: Server logs are piped directly into the TUI, so I can catch 404s or 500s instantly. 3. Automated Cleanup: A wrapper script (`manage_site.sh`) ensures that whenever I close the app, all background processes are killed.

How it Works

The backend uses Python's `subprocess` module to manage the server process. It captures the PID and uses `os.killpg` to ensure the entire process group is terminated when requested.


def stop_server(self):
    os.killpg(os.getpgid(self.process.pid), 15)
Now, I have a single "Command Center" for my entire blog workflow.

item.title: The Ultimate Workflow: Going Headless
item.content:encoded:
We just spent the morning iterating on how to manage this blog. It was a perfect example of how developer tools evolve—and where they are going.

The Evolution of Friction

Phase 1: The Terminal Mess

At first, I was managing everything manually.

  • One terminal tab for `python -m http.server`.
  • Another for `git`.
  • Another for editing files.
  • Result: I had 6+ orphaned processes running in the background and constantly had to `kill` ports. It was messy.

Phase 2: The TUI (Site Manager)

To fix this, we built a custom Site Manager TUI.

  • It had a dashboard.
  • It had buttons to Start/Stop the server.
  • It had a log viewer.
  • Result: It was better, but it was still another tool I had to manage. I had to navigate tabs, press keys, and deal with UI glitches (like mouse clicks not working).

Phase 3: Going Headless

Then we realized something important: I don't want to manage the server. I just want the server to work.

So we deleted the UI.

Now, I have a "Headless" workflow. I simply tell my AI assistant:

"Make a post about X"
"Publish my changes"

The AI handles the processes, the git commands, and the file edits in the background. I don't see a terminal. I don't see a dashboard. I just see the results.

Why This Matters

This is the future of interfaces. We often build GUIs (Graphical User Interfaces) because we need a way to control complex systems. But if we have an intelligent agent that understands our intent, the best UI is no UI at all.

I'm not clicking buttons anymore. I'm just creating.

item.title: The Quiet Return of RSS in 2025
item.description: In an age of algorithmic noise, the simple XML feed has become a sanctuary for intentional consumption.
item.content:encoded:
It is 2025, and the most advanced technology in my reading stack is a protocol from 1999.

For a decade, we were told that RSS was dead. Google Reader died, and with it, we were herded into the walled gardens of social media. We traded control for convenience. We traded chronology for "engagement."

But the pendulum is swinging back.

The Great Algorithmic Fatigue

By the mid-2020s, the novelty of the "For You" page had worn off. We realized that an algorithm designed to maximize time-on-site does not necessarily maximize quality of life.

We became tired of being fed rage-bait. Tired of AI-generated slop filling our feeds. Tired of not seeing the posts from the actual humans we chose to follow.

In this noise, RSS (Really Simple Syndication) re-emerged not as a retro curiosity, but as a tool of survival.

Intentional Consumption

Using an RSS reader in 2025 is a radical act of intentionality. It says: "I choose what I see. I choose when I see it. And when I am done, I am done."

There is no infinite scroll in an RSS reader. There is a "Mark All As Read" button. There is a finish line. That psychological difference is profound.

AI Agents and Structured Data

There is another, more technical reason for RSS's resurgence: AI Agents.

As we move from browsing the web to having agents browse for us, structured data becomes gold. An AI agent doesn't want to scrape a heavy, ad-laden HTML page to find out if I wrote a new article. It wants a clean, machine-readable XML feed.

RSS is the API of the open web.

When you publish an RSS feed, you aren't just letting humans subscribe. You are future-proofing your content for a world where consumption is increasingly mediated by intelligent software.

A Sovereign Connection

Ultimately, RSS is about sovereignty. It is a direct pipe between the writer and the reader. No middleman. No shadowbanning. No pay-to-play reach.

If you are reading this, you can click the small WiFi-like icon in the header of this site. Add it to your reader. You won't get a notification when I post unless you ask for it. You won't be manipulated into clicking.

You will just get the words, exactly as I wrote them, whenever you are ready to read them.

And in 2025, that feels like a luxury.

item.title: The Return of Personal Software
item.description: For decades, we've been renters in someone else's digital house. But the ability to build desktop apps for personal use is bringing back something we forgot we lost—software sovereignty.
item.content:encoded:
There is a quiet revolution happening in the spaces between commercial software releases.

For the past thirty years, we've been trained to think of software as something that comes in shrink-wrapped boxes (or app stores, if you're under 40). Someone builds it. We buy it. We learn to work within its limitations. If it doesn't do what we need, we wait for version 2.0 or we switch to a competitor.

We became renters in someone else's digital house.

But something is shifting. The ability to build desktop apps—real, native applications—for personal use is no longer the domain of professional developers. It's becoming a basic literacy. And it's changing the way we think about what software is for.

The Lost Vernacular

In the early days of personal computing, building your own software was common. Not because people were all programmers, but because the tools were simple enough and the problems were personal enough that tinkering was natural.

You didn't buy a spreadsheet to calculate your taxes. You opened VisiCalc or Lotus 1-2-3 and built the exact calculation you needed. You didn't buy project management software. You hacked together a HyperCard stack that worked the way you thought about projects.

There was a vernacular computing culture—people building small, weird, personal tools that solved their specific problems in their specific way.

Then software became an industry. Tools became products. Products became platforms. And the vernacular disappeared.

The Shift

Today, the barriers to building personal software have collapsed in two ways:

1. The tools are better. Frameworks like Electron, Tauri, and Swift make it trivial to package a web interface into a native desktop app. You don't need to understand Windows APIs or Cocoa frameworks. If you can build a webpage, you can build an app.

2. The intelligence is embedded. AI-assisted development tools (like the one I used to build this blog) have made it possible to describe what you want and have most of the tedious work done for you. You become a director, not a typist.

The result: building a desktop app for personal use is now a weekend project, not a career.

What Does "Personal Software" Look Like?

It's not trying to be for everyone. It's hyper-specific.

  • A media organizer that works exactly the way you categorize things, not the way Apple Photos thinks you should.
  • A note-taking app that mirrors your actual thought process, not someone's idea of "productivity."
  • A budgeting tool that handles your weird financial situation (freelance income, crypto holdings, that one rental property) instead of forcing you into someone else's categories.

Personal software is opinionated. It reflects your preferences, your workflow, your aesthetics.

It doesn't need to scale. It doesn't need to onboard new users. It doesn't need a roadmap or a changelog.

It just needs to work for you.

The Politics of Software Sovereignty

There's something subtly radical about building your own tools.

When you use commercial software, you are subject to its politics. The company decides what features matter. They decide when to deprecate old functionality. They decide whether to sell your data or raise the subscription price.

You are a guest.

When you build your own software, you are sovereign. You decide the features. You decide the lifespan. You own the data.

This isn't just a technical shift. It's a shift in power.

The Invitation

You don't need to become a "developer" to participate in this. You just need to start thinking of software not as a product you consume, but as a material you shape.

Build a small tool. Make it ugly. Make it personal. Make it yours.

The desktop is not dead. It's just been waiting for you to remember that it belongs to you.
